This is really about whether Intel can turn roadmap progress into real, repeatable manufacturing execution.

On Stocktwits, bullish tags outnumber bearish 24 to 10, with users calling it a 'skyrocket' and comparing its intraday valuation to Microsoft. The excitement is spilling over to other space stocks like ASTS and RKLB, which also have extremely bullish sentiment.

AI and semiconductor stocks continue to dominate. NVDA has a 28:2 bullish-to-bearish ratio on Stocktwits, and messages highlight its $20B bond offering and AI dominance. MU and AVGO are also seeing positive chatter, with a CNBC analyst quoted saying MU and SNDK have pricing power for 2-3 years. Meanwhile, market ETFs SPY and QQQ remain in focus, with QQQ showing strong bullish sentiment (20:6) despite some geopolitical concerns.

The crowd is divided on Tesla and Netflix. TSLA has more bearish tags (17) than bullish (13), with messages like 'dumping continues' and a bearish options signal. In contrast, NFLX saw a massive spike in mentions from 8 to 75, likely due to earnings, but sentiment data is not available. This tension suggests that while the overall market is bullish, there are pockets of skepticism that could lead to volatility.

overlooked_insights.title: SpaceX Filed as Data Service, Not Rocket Company
overlooked_insights.insight: Multiple Stocktwits users claim SpaceX did not file as a space company for its IPO but as a data processing service, because 93% of its addressable market is AI, not rockets. This would fundamentally change valuation frameworks if true, yet no headline confirms or denies the filing classification.
overlooked_insights.evidence: User writes: 'SpaceX did NOT file as a space company. They filed as a data processing service because 93% of their total addressable market is AI, not rockets.'
overlooked_insights.why_overlooked: Mainstream coverage focuses on the rocket/moonshot narrative and ignores the actual SEC filing category, which could redefine peer comparisons.

overlooked_insights.title: Tesla Holds 19M SpaceX Shares
overlooked_insights.insight: A user posted that as of May 1, 2026, Tesla owns exactly 18,990,195 shares of SpaceX Class A common stock (less than 1% of post-IPO shares). This cross-ownership is a material hidden asset within Tesla that no headline discusses.
overlooked_insights.evidence: User quote: 'Tesla (TSLA) owns 18,990,195 shares of SpaceX Class A common stock (as of May 1, 2026).'
overlooked_insights.why_overlooked: Mainstream Tesla coverage centers on AI5 chips, robotaxis, and BYD competition, not the value of its SpaceX stake.

SNAP.summary: The online chatter around Snap (SNAP) is buzzing, mainly because of the recent reveal of their new AR glasses, Spectacles 5, priced at $2,195. People are split: some see it as a gimmick that won't move the needle, while others think it's a game-changer that could finally give Snap a new revenue stream. The stock has been in a long-term downtrend, and many investors are frustrated, calling it a "bleeding" stock. However, the spike in Reddit mentions (from 1 to 15 in 24 hours) shows that the AR glasses launch has reignited interest. On Stocktwits, bullish and bearish tags are almost even (13 vs 12), reflecting the uncertainty. Some users are sarcastic about selling expensive glasses when the core business is struggling, but others argue that early adopters will pay a premium, and the glasses could be a hit for watching shows discreetly. There's also speculation about a potential buyout from Apple, Tesla, or Meta, given the advanced tech. But skeptics point out that previous Spectacles added zero revenue, and the company was profitable without them, so why would this time be different? Overall, the community is watching closely to see if pre-order numbers can justify the hype. Investors should know that while the AR glasses are exciting, Snap's core advertising business faces headwinds from competition and privacy changes. The stock is volatile, and the next earnings report will be crucial.
